title: 2026/03/16 - TI
displayTitle: 2026-03-15 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Building (Earthquake-prone Buildings) Amendment Bill
description: 16 March 2026 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Building (Earthquake-prone Buildings) Amendment Bill Manawatu District Council Michael Ford, Mayor Te Hiranga Ru QuakeCoRE Professor Santiago Pujol Zoran Rakovic Su-Wuen Ong ICOMOS NZ Chessa Stevens Stacy Vallis, President, ICOMOS Aotearoa New Zealand National Committee
descriptionSummary: Building (Earthquake-prone Buildings) Amendment Bill. Manawatu District Council. Michael Ford, Mayor

chunks.text: Right. Are we all there?

Not quite. Just waiting on Simon Court. Here he is. Good. Good to go, Mr. Chair. Okay, thank you. Right. Um, Liam. Yes. Who have we got first up then? Uh, Michael Ford from Manawatū District Council. Okay. Oh, right, great stuff. Okay. Michael, are you there?

He's just coming in now. I've just invited him. Okay, cool. Yep. Mr. Chair, can you just confirm your members are present? I can only see you at the moment, which is the function of the dev- I, I can see Simon Court, Carlos Chan- Yeah. Andy Foster, myself.

I can't see Carlos. I can see that he's... Carlos, yeah. Carlos, you there? I can see Carlos. Yeah, all good. Yep, we've got everyone there. Thank you, Tracy. Right, okay. Can we see you, Michael? Uh, Michael just needs to accept the promotion to be a panelist. We'll try again.
Here he comes.

You know, this would all be so much easier if, leave were granted on Thursdays when we were in sitting times to, actually meet. Well, you don't have to go into urgency. Just making the point. It's not urgency, it's just extended hours. Mm-hmm. Well, same thing. But just, just make the point it'd be so much easier. Right. We wouldn't be shuffling around between devices.

It's your, your call, not ours. Well, it's not our call, sadly, but never mind. And also, we might need to speak in the house, so it's no good. Well, maybe. Maybe. Well, welcome Michael. Michael. Mr. Chairman, I don't know if you can see me, but, um- Can't, can't see you.

I can hear you. Can you hear me? Uh, yes, loud and clear. Can, can you hear me? Yes. Okay. Well, I'm not sure why the ca- the camera's not working. It seems to be, but, I think I should con- make a start. Yeah. Shall I? Okay, look, let's, let's get, let's get going. I'll give you the usual story.

Um, we've got you there for 10 minutes. Um- Right. Can, can you hear me? Yes. Can you, can you hear me, folks? Yeah, okay. So Michael, you should be able to hear us. Yes. We can hear you. Loud and clear. Yeah. Okay. So you've got 10 minutes, 10 minutes- All right... starting from now. Thanks very much. Look, good morning, Chair, Andy Foster, and members of the...

and, and members of the select committee. I'm Michael Ford, the mayor of Manawatū District. And I thank you for this opportunity. I'll take out a moment- Um, Michael... and just comment on- Michael... one or two points. Michael. Um- Michael... Manawatū Council and community is very supportive of this bill.

Better. It affects us greatly. Uh, we've been advocating for years for legislation that better balances proportional risk to human life with affordability. Yes. Yes. Yes? No, you ... Michael, it's just you're coming and going a little bit, but it was getting better then.

Okay. As soon as I stopped, I'm trying to ask you. Can you hear me? Yeah. No, keep going. Can you tell? Yes. Keep going. All right. I'll carry on. Um, thank you. Uh, so yeah, we've been, we've been advocating for years. Uh, we ran the real risk that Fielding, our most, our most earthquake.

We're in a high risk zone and have had short time frames to strengthen or demolish. Our main town is Fielding, with a population of 18,000 people. Fielding is 20 kilometers away from Palmerston North, and as such, has a much smaller town center than towns with similar population further from a major city.

We don't have a major state highway running through our town center. There are likely more people and vehicles along the footpath... Mi- Michael. Roads of- Michael. Michael, you ... It's really breaking up. I think what we might do is to have another crack at this, shortly, colleagues.

Is just time? Yeah. Yeah, M- Michael.Can we, can we get you back on? I, I don't know where you are in terms of, in terms of reception, but it's not very good and you're breaking up a lot. Oh, Mr. Chair. Yes. Can we just ask the submitter to use the phone calling, call one? Maybe it's easier.

Liam, can we do that? Uh, yeah. We can send some details to the submitter. Okay. Michael, what, what we'll do is if we can get you back on, later on this morning, then we'll do that in a way which we can actually hear you, because you're breaking up a lot. Did you hear that, Michael?

I think we might have lost him entirely anyway. Uh, Liam, is, is Michael still there? Uh, he is still there, but I haven't heard anything come through either, so... Okay. Look, can you, can you liaise with Michael and we will, we will find another time for him, and we'll try, just try and do it in a way that we can actually hear him and it's not breaking up all the time.
Yes. We'll bring the next submitter in now. Okay. That would be great. Thank you.

Good morning. Morning. I, I hope I have better signal than the previous speaker. Well, yes, you're sounding better. Yeah. Okay. Cool. Soran. No, Santiago. Sorry. My apologies. Okay. Right. Um, we've got you down for, check Liam, is this ten minutes?

Yes, that's right. Okay, ten minutes. I'll give you a call with about four to go. Um, and, you can take your submission as read. Uh, but if you wish to, highlight any points, that's great. So we... I, I'll start your time now. Thank you very much. I have some slides that are not, necessarily required, but, they, they could help. Um, the first point I'd like to make is, here representing QuakeCoRE, Centre of Research Excellence, funded by TEC, and our main goal is to improve earthquake resilience

in Aotearoa, New Zealand. Uh, we're a large organization with more than 150 researchers, and affiliates, and as many students. So I have the challenge to try to communicate the ideas of this big community in a few, brief points. The first one I like to make is that, we think the bill shouldn't restrict TAs to identify the most vulnerable buildings out there. The bill is right in that it targets the worst of the worst, and that's quite welcome because, what we had was leading to

inaction. Uh, but the bill shouldn't restrict the ability of TAs to identify some of these, worst buildings, namely URM, unreinforced masonry, and multi-story pre-1976 concrete buildings after, roughly, 2027. There's a clause, clause number 133i (1) , that would restrict a TA from identifying some of these buildings, and I'll come back to that point because I want to emphasize

it. Um, but in the meantime, let me also mention that, we think the government has to lead by example and, and the best way to do that is to address, critical buildings, buildings that are important for emergency response, such as hospitals and, fire stations.

Uh, school buildings are also key and, and the bill sort of de-emphasizes what's going on in that space. Uh, in fact, the, parliament paper that came out before the bill sort of makes, the point that fewer of such buildings are going to be addressed with the bill, but somehow, that doesn't come through in the bill.

Oh, I see I got a text that says I can use my slides, so let me switch to that. Uh, if I choose the right screen. You should be seeing my slides, and I'm trying to make here the second point, that, there should be more emphasis on buildings that are critical for emergency response or at least, there should be some effort to clarify what's going to be done to address such buildings. Uh, the next point I'd like to make is that, in the new, methodology and, and, and the new regulation, there are very different types of retrofits that would be

mandated. Um, going all the way from just fixing a facade in a URM building to doing what the bill calls full retrofit, which is much more comprehensive. Well, as it stands, the time frames that are given to achieve those very different types of retrofits with very different scopes are the same.

That doesn't make sense to us. We think that it would take much less time to fix a facade than it would take to fix an entire building, so the, time frame should be tiered depending on the scope. And related to that, and I'm gonna, sort of condense two points into one here, there should be some effort to improve the terminology in the bill, and that should go together with a, sort of clarification or, or informational campaign.And I'll give two examples here. Uh, to say that Auckland has low risk, it's, it's misleading and a little bit,

counterproductive. See, Auckland and, and the Northland might have lower hazard, but because of the density of the, built environment there in, in... You could argue it has a higher risk. And, low, it's, it's, it should be put in relative terms.

It's the hazard in the north is lower than in other regions, but not necessarily low, especially if you consider sort of what happens, internationally. And last but not least, the national and international evidence shows that, mandatory measures only go so far unless there are some, some incentives, usually, financial incentives in the form of lower taxes or access to low interest rates. Uh, 'cause otherwise it might be very difficult for people to,

act. I see there is a question. Yeah, we'll deal with questions when you're finished. Okay, great. You've got, you've got about, um... We're almo- coming up five and a half minutes at the moment. Yeah. Yeah. Thank you. So I'd like to go back to that first point because, we believe it's quite important. Uh, I'm sure everyone here remembers this building, the Pine Gold Corporation building or PGC building that, caused the tragic death of 18 people in twenty eleven.

This is sort of the type of building we are after, other than URM buildings. This is a reinforced concrete building, a multi-story built before 1976. And as late as 2007, the building was classified to have a percent NBS of two-thirds, sixty-seven percent. And this quote, which was extracted from the Royal Commission report, again, as late as 2007, said the building wasn't earthquake prone. And in fact, the report goes on to quote, that, the building was

expected to behave well. And we're trying to make this point here to show that the methods that were being used to classify buildings before the Canterbury earthquakes and before changes that were made in 2017, that didn't always work. Uh, at least they didn't always produce consistent results. And that's one of the reasons why we're introducing a new methodology. Uh, so clause, again, one thirty-three i (1) - You've got about three minutes left.

Yeah. Clause one thirty-three i (1) restricts TAs who may come into or, or come across buildings like this to address them after twenty twenty-seven or after the, time frames expire. Uh, and that we think is problematic. We think the TAs should have the opportunity to address, buildings a-as bad as this one in case they run into them, whether, it's, through a consent request or, a review triggered maybe by a change in use, and so

on. And I think I have two minutes left, so I'm gonna leave those, to try to answer questions. Uh, look, thank you. Really appreciate that. So, colleagues, are there any questions? Celia. Jura, thank you for a very interesting submission. I had two questions about things that...

You're very clear about your recommendations. Um, any view on the change to Dunedin's status? You mentioned Auckland, and I don't see much commentary about apartment buildings. I wondered if you had any thoughts on either of those points. Yeah. For Dunedin, I personally think, the change is, quite timely. Uh, there is evidence that there are faults south of Dunedin, m- sort of oriented towards Dunedin, creating a, a situation not too different from what, happened here in Christchurch in twenty eleven. So I, I think, I think Dunedin in a

way is overdue for, retrofit, urgent retrofit. And then in terms of apartment buildings, yeah, they should be a main concern, especially because it may be very difficult for apartment owners to agree on how to act to retrofit an entire building. Uh, we're doing a lot of research to try to find efficient ways to retrofit, ways to minimize disruption, because it's precisely the concern that you, you don't want tenants or, owners to have to evacuate for long periods of time.

It should be a priority. Okay. Um, just check. Uh, any other last question, anybody? Uh, if, if other questions come up, please send me, um- We, we, we will do that. Look, can I ask just one very quick one? Just with respect to the, the PGG building that you talk about.

So I'm presuming what you were referring to is the, what's referred to as the anytime pathway and just making sure that that is not too difficult for TAs- Right... to pick, to pick things up. But what we've been told is that the problem's been that, councils have been tending to pick up, being very risk averse and picking up all sorts of buildings which are probably relatively ro- low risk.

So we've got to try and get that right. Is that what you're saying? Yeah, the, you have to sort of strike the right balance there. But in, in my mind, that any time pathway should, also be allowed for these older buildings which are the, the most vulnerable, the, the, the ones that worry us the most. Uh, right now it, it would be open only for newer buildings and, in my mind, doesn't make a whole lot ... that doesn't make a whole lot of sense.

Okay, look, we really appreciate that, so thank you very much for all the detailed submission. Thank you for the opportunity. Thank you. Right. Uh, we go to Zoran Rakovic. Zoran, are you there? And- Zoran's are on through now. Okay. Liam, can you just tell us who else is, in the meeting room at the moment, in terms of moving on to next?

Uh, after that we have Suwen Ong and Ekomos NZ. Right, okay. So everybody's in the meeting room. Yes. And we'll see if we can get, Michael Foreback. Michael Foreback. That would be great. Brilliant. Okay, thank you. Right, Zoran, are you there? Uh, yes. Good morning. Good morning. Uh- Good morning... welcome. Welcome. We've got you down for five minutes.

I'll give you a call with, about two minutes to go. Um, we've got your submission, you can take that as read. Um, but, your time starts now. Uh, thank you, chair and members of the committee for the opportunity to speak. Uh, my name is Zoran Rakovic. I'm a retired structural engineer.

I support the direction this bill is taking, but w- I would like to make four practical recommendations. First one is related to percent MBS rating. It's been with us engineers, councils, building owners, and banks, lenders, and others for quite some time now, and just abruptly ending it, I think could cause some, discomfort. And what I'm proposing is that we keep that in parallel with the new, new grading system for a limited period of time, maybe three to five years, so people get used to new grading. I'm pretty sure that, if we

drop it immediately, clients would start asking, "Oh, how does that compare with MBS?" And we would be doing that in parallel anyway. Uh, second, when we do seismic retrofit of buildings, we usually or sometimes add columns, braces, thicken walls, and thing... do things that could affect fire rating or fire escape routes. Um, I therefore recommend that instead of just, completely abandoning fire engineering check, we do one before the retrofit is done, and then do fire engineering checks during design or when the building's

completed just to make sure that none of these new elements have, altered fire escape routes or fire rating. Sometimes our elements penetrate through firewalls or, or, connect to fire-rated elements and that, that needs to be looked at. I also believe that, many of these, older and vulnerable buildings are owned by people or companies, with, limited funds or, or limited cash flow. What I'm proposing is that, consenting for retrofit is done once, but owners all- are allowed to do, sensible

staging of the building works so they can deliver it in a, in a more friendly, cash flow friendly manner. Um, my recommendation is that the council gives one consent, but then gives code compliance certificate for each stage completed. Uh, I also believe that, there's need for a better communication between building owners who, a- are, responsible for the building, a- and, and the tenants, communities that use the buildings so that those people who are actually

affected by the building's earthquake performance are kept informed as to what's happening, whether a retrofit's gonna be done, and, and the timing of such works. In summary, I support the bill, and these are my four recommendations. Thank, thank you for your time and opportunity to speak.

Look, thank you, Zoran. Very, very useful. Um, in terms of questions, I'll ask two quick ones and if anybody else has got one, that'd be great. So with, with respect to the second recommendation you had about, fire walls, et cetera, essentially you're just saying, "Don't ma- just make sure you don't make it worse." Where if the build doesn't require you upgrading, but at least you should have an obligation to make sure it doesn't make the fire safety worse.

Uh, well, I'm not a fire engineer. I believe that's the intent of the bill. And, you know, I cannot commend ... co- comment on the merit of that, but what I ca- the, the idea is that don't forget that building works, seismic building works could affect fire rating, and if that's the intent of the build, then a check needs to be done.

Okay. That's, that's, that's clear. Uh, the second one is, that you talked about the consenting being done once, but then allowing for owners to do the work in stages if they chose. Um, is that not normally the case now? Or the, or the consents, the consents lapse and, you know, you've done part of it or, is it just too difficult at the moment?

Uh, I think it's too difficult at the moment. Normally, from my experience of, retrofitting buildings, you get a consent and you've got to do it in one go. Uh, there is no, there is no e- e- act, the building act does not have a built-in staging allowance, and if you build it into this bill or this, yeah, amendment, I think it could help owners have some, something to talk about with the councils.

Okay. That's useful. And also- See, very quick, very quick question. Yep. I beg your pardon? You're, you're on mute. Who, me? No, no, no. No, Celier is. Sorry. Sorry. Right. Um, it's also on the fire area. The fire safety assessment before and after, would you, expect those to be lodged on the, the building information memorandum at the council?

Uh, how I would approach it is I would get the fire engineer to do assessment of the building before I start my design, and then as I develop my design, I would check it with the fire engineer that I'm not affecting anything. And when the consent is lodged, that's when the f- second fire report would be included to demonstrate to the council that no, no, adverse effect is, you know, that there is no adverse effect on fire- That's- Zoran, thank you very much. We really appreciate it.
So thank you for some very clear- Okay... suggestions. Thank you for your time. Thank you. Okay. Now we've got, Su Wen Ong. Su? Su Wen, are you there?
Liam, we've got Su Wen. Yeah. Su Wen just needs to unmute and, turn the, camera on. She's un-unmuting now. Su Wen, welcome. Can you unmute, please? You're still on mute.

Okay. Right, Su Wen. Thank you. Uh, we've got your submission, which you can take as read. Um, we've got you down for five minutes, so if you want to highlight any key points, and, well, I mean, you've obviously made one very key point, then, please do, and I'll give you a call with about two minutes to go. If, we can leave some time for questions, it will be great.
So your time starts now.

Su, we can't hear you. I'll stop the, I'll stop the clock. Are you, are you ... Is this a, a thing if you got your volume down at zero or? Have a look at your volume, either your settings or your, or your volume on your-

Uh, Mr. Chair, if, if you wanna give Su Wen a moment to sort out the audio issue, we do have Michael Ford back if you wanted to go to them. Okay. Su Wen, what we'll do is we'll let you sort your, audio out. We'll, we'll come back to you with the full five minutes, momentarily when that- we've got that sorted, and we'll go to one of the other submitters. Okay. Michael.

Just bring Michael through again now. Okay. Right. Thank you. Michael, welcome back. Thank you. And we can see you as well. My goodness, that's a, that's a shock for you. Hey, lovely to see you again. Um, we'll start your, we'll start your time from scratch, because we really, you know, it was, really c-cutting in and out. Um, and so, you can take it as, you know, normal rules. You take everything as read and, in your time.

Oh, thank, thanks very much, and apologies for the technology. Oh, it happens. I'll just make it o- call it operator error. Um, so good morning again, Chair, Andy Foster and the, and members of the Select Committee. I'm Michael Ford, Mayor of Manawatu, and I thank you for this opportunity. Take our submission as read and just comment on one or two points. The Manawatu Council and community is very supportive of this bill. Uh, we've been advocating for many years for legislation that better balances proportional risk to human life with

affordability, and we ran the real risk that Fielding's most attractive town center would be destroyed by legislation or policy rather than an earthquake. We are in a high risk zone and have had short timeframes to strengthen or demolish. Our main town is Fielding. It has a population of eighteen thousand. Fielding is only twenty kilometers from Palmerston North, and as such, has a much smaller town center than towns with, similar populations further from a major city.

And we don't have a major state highway running through our town center. There are likely more people in vehicles on the footpaths and towns of, such places as Taihape and Dannevirke, which are more standalone towns on major state highways, but with populations under ten thousand. This means that owners of Fielding's unreinforced, reinforced masonry buildings, which make up a significant percentage of our town center, have to secure facades, and the towns with smaller populations don't. Our council accepts this, but is looking for comparative relief in terms of timeframes.

We don't wanna be hit with a double whammy by comparison with similar but smaller towns. Standard templates for engineering solutions to secure facades would be very helpful, similar to what we understand was available in Wellington, the Hutt Valley, Kaikoura, et cetera, after the Kaikoura earthquake, albeit with considerable funding.Before Minister Chris Pink's extensions, Fielding building owners had until March two thousand and twenty-eight.

It's only two years away to strengthen or demolish the priority buildings which are most of the town center unreinforced masonry buildings. This is because our council established priority thoroughfares, unwisely in my opinion, some years ago, which reduced the timeframe from fifteen years to seven and a half years to strengthen or demolish.

Twelve buildings have been strengthened, which is good, which were the low-hanging fruit. We're struggling with the others. We now have until March two thousand and thirty-two, whereas towns without priority thoroughfares have at least another seven and a half years. We're aware of the proposal to give councils discretion to extend the timeframe, which we support, but we just want a more level playing field.

What would help would be a clear interpretation of what sufficient vehicle or pedestrian traffic to warrant prioritization means. What does the word sufficient mean? A particular pedestrian count or traffic count would be helpful, and I refer you to the bottom of, of our submission pages, bottom of page nine, ten, eleven, and the top of page twelve. Um, we would like to revisit priority thoroughfares and be able to reissue earthquake-prone building notices, building notices with revised remediation timeframes, and we would welcome the removal of the difference in

timeframes between priority buildings and non-priority buildings in relatively small towns such as ours. We're not gonna comment on the larger cities. We're just wanting to be the same, the same timeframes as our neighbors. Thank you for this opportunity. Michael, thank you very much. Um, questions, colleagues.

Can I ask you one, Michael? I mean, you've, you've invited us to, to measure the size of towns quite differently and also to have another think about, clarity around, pedestrian and traffic counts. Do you have any thoughts about the practicalities of doing that in a way which was robust? Because whatever methodology you use, it's gonna affect building owners.

Um, you're either in or you're out, you know, you're on one side of a line or not on one si- or on the other side of a line. Um, that's quite, important thing, so it needs to be a robust, a very robust regime. Yeah, look- Got, got any thoughts about that? In, in terms of the, the larger population or the ten thousand threshold, we're, we're just accepting that, but we're just point-pointing out, that we are probably, even though we've got eighteen thousand people, we probably, have as many people in our town centers as,

as, a lot s- smaller towns. What we're just looking for is we really want to undo the priority thoroughfare. Um, I mean, if we could just remove the difference in timeframes between, those with priority thoroughfares and those that don't, or priority buildings and those that don't, that would probably be all that we're looking for.

Was that not something that you said... You said it was unwize, in your view, an unwise decision, but it was a decision made by the council? It, it was a decision of our, a previous council, yes, and- So is there anything to prevent that being the, the council review, revisiting that decision? Uh, well, the issue was more we... Well, up until now, we weren't able to undo the, the building notices that were sent. So yes, we could rescind that decision, but it wasn't going to affect the building notices that were sent.

And so we're wanting to ensure that whatever we do, we can actually change the, the actual timeframes that building owners have been given. Um, and one way of doing that would be if the legislation just removed the difference in timeframe between, those with pri-priority build, or the priority buildings and non-priority buildings.

Okay. That's, that, that's helpful to understand that. Right. Any other questions, colleagues? I can't see any hands up. No, Michael, I think that's, that's very helpful, and it's given us a, a little thing to think about there. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thanks for the opportunity. And, and Fielding is often the poster child for, these, these issues around seismic, seismic discussions.

Thank you very much. So point to well made. Thank you. Okay. Bye-bye. Right. We'll go back to Su Wen Ong, if we've got the technology working there. We'll try bringing Su Wen on again. Okay. Thank you.

Have we got Su Wen yet? Oh, yes, I see you there. Okay. Right. We'll have another go this time, Su Wen. Hopefully, the technology will work for you. Um, you're on, you're on mute at the moment, so we'll take... If we can take the mute off, then hopefully we've got the volume.

You're still on mute, Su Wen. Okay. I think... Can we try it, can we try it by phone, Liam? How do... Uh, can you make that work for us? Yes, we'll send Su Wen the, the dial-in details. Did you wanna move to Akamos, in the meantime? I think, I think we'll do that, yes.
Su Wen, what, what we're gonna do is move to another submitter and, our staff will give you a call and, and we'll do it by phone.Okay? Right, okay, we'll, we'll put ICOMOS on then.

Right. Have we got ICOMOS there? Chessa? Good morning. Good morning. We can hear you loud and clear, that's great. Excellent . Okay, so good, we've been having a few technical problems. So, right, okay, we've got you down for 10 minutes. Um, you can take your submission as read, and thank you for all the work and the expertise that you bring to this, this issue.

Um, and I'll give you a call with about four minutes to go in terms of leaving some time for questions, so, over to you. Okay, great. Stacy's gonna kick us off. Sure. Thank you very much- Hi, Stacy... Kia ora. Uh, kia ora, Andy. Um, to the chair, and also to the virtual audience, I'm Stacy Vallis, the present chair of ICOMOS Aotearoa New Zealand, and a lecturer in architecture, and I'm here this morning with my team, including Chessa, the chair of our legislation and policy team, and, and, and a heritage conservation

architect. So ICOMOS is a non-governmental, organization focused on the heritage conservation of the world's monuments and sites. Founded in 1965, ICOMOS is a principal advisor to UNESCO, and includes over 10,000 members in 132 nations, spread across the globe. The ICOMOS New Zealand charter is the benchmark for heritage conservation standards and practice in, Aotearoa New Zealand. So this morning we'd like to thank you for taking the time to read our submission, and also offering us the chance to share some feedback on the draft

bill. We note that the purpose of the bill is to set up a more proportionist and risk-based regulatory system for managing our earthquake-prone buildings. So of interest to us are the ways in which the bills, the, the bill will impact the owners, occupiers, and communities with an interest, in these heritage buildings and structures.

So I'll now hand over, to my team, to Chessa, to run us through a few of our key points. So as an organization, ICOMOS Aotearoa New Zealand supports removing the current method of identifying and assessing earthquake-prone buildings based on the extent to which they have to achieve the new building standard or percent MBS, and the underpi- underpinning initial evaluation procedure, or IEP, that is used to calculate this. Uh, for reasons that are highlighted in our submission, we believe the current system has led to inaccurate or unnecessarily

pessimistic ratings, particularly for historic buildings. We support the tiered mitigation requirements proposed that will target retrofitting and/or remove the need to retrofit some building types, as we agree that this will better reflect the actual level of risk of individual buildings and will be more cost effective.

We support removing the requirement to comply with Section 112 provisions of the Building Act relating to means of escape from fire and accessibility as nearly as reasonably practicable, because we agree that this will reduce the cost. However, this does not mean that any implications of strengthening on a building's existing fire safety or accessibility provisions should be ignored, rather that a basic requirement not to make the existing provisions worse may be implemented without any need for additional upgrading. Lastly, we support

enabling territorial authorities to grant deadline extensions for seismic works to ensure that building owners have sufficient time to carry out remediation. Uh, notwithstanding that overall we do support the bill, ICOMOS Aotearoa New Zealand have some concerns, which Stacy will now run you through.

Thanks. So we would like to note that the proposed, reassignment of coastal Otago and Dunedin and part of Stewart Island from a low seismic risk to a medium seismic zone will impact a significant number of heritage buildings, particularly masonry buildings. Even with the proposed tiered approach, many heritage buildings will still be impacted, and these buildings will need bespoke solutions. So we'd like to strongly promote that specific guidance for heritage buildings be prepared by experts in historic building conservation,

and that they are made available by organizations, including Heritage New Zealand Māori Tānga, MBIE, and TAs. Furthermore, the provision of financial support to building owners in the form of grants or similar would also help speed up the rate at which the works that will still be required by the bill take place, and this will aid the building owners as well as the centers in which the buildings are located, and also s- help support wider national economic growth. So we'd like to strongly promote the, the, reinstatement of the Heritage Equip Fund,

which is, which had supported the strengthening of multiple heritage buildings and the investigation and design work that informs strengthening, as well as providing increased funding to and by, TAs and Heritage New Zealand to allow them to create or increase financial grants and/or relief programs for strengthening heritage buildings. So I'll now hand over to Chessa to close us off.

Thanks, Chessa.
